name: Superposition Principle
summary: For linear systems, the net response at any point is the sum of the individual responses — waves, fields, and quantum states all obey superposition.
whenToUseIt: Use superposition whenever you have multiple sources (waves, charges, forces) contributing to a single effect and the system is linear.
steps: Confirm the system obeys linear equations (most wave and field equations do)
Calculate the effect of each source individually
Add all individual contributions together (vector sum for forces/fields, algebraic sum for scalar waves)
Analyse the combined result for interference patterns, nodes, or net fields
Check boundary conditions are satisfied by the combined solution
example: Two speakers emit sound waves of the same frequency. At some points, the peaks align (constructive interference) and the sound is loud. At other points, a peak meets a trough (destructive interference) and the sound cancels. This is superposition in action.
